ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken bolognese; 'pasta dish' (FOODON_00002473) is the closest defensible parent class. Broader match applied as closest_parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers pasta dishes broadly; no specific chicken bolognese code exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken bolognese as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chicken bolognese with spaghetti is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of cooked pasta and 150 g of chicken tomato sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Bolognese — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Bolognese (cr_id F05060025), confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for spaghetti with meat sauce (closest analogue) and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken bolognese as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked spaghetti (white, boiled) has a published GI of approximately 45–49 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The tomato-based sauce and protein content from minced chicken further moderate the glycaemic response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 12.77 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 25.4 for a 350 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 255 mg per 100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 893 mg sodium, representing ~39% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request low-sodium preparation, avoid adding salt at the table, and use low-sodium canned tomatoes to keep a 350 g serving below 600 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 12.77 g per 100 g with a low estimated GI of 45; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 44.7 g carbohydrate, which requires portion awareness.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 250–300 g, request wholegrain pasta to lower the GI further, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 5.4 g per 100 g with estimated saturated fat ~1.3 g per 100 g; sodium at 255 mg per 100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern at typical serving sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should choose this dish over cream-based pasta alternatives, request olive oil-based preparation, and monitor sodium intake by limiting portion size to 250 g or requesting reduced-salt cooking.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomato sauce contains solanine alkaloids (nightshade family) that may exacerbate joint inflammation in solanine-sensitive RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients sensitive to nightshades should avoid or monitor symptoms carefully; consider substituting tomato sauce with a non-nightshade alternative such as pumpkin or butternut squash sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken bolognese is a Western-style pasta dish consisting of boiled spaghetti topped with a slow-simmered sauce made from minced chicken, tomatoes, onion, garlic, olive oil, and herbs. It is a lighter adaptation of the traditional Italian beef bolognese, substituting minced chicken for red meat to reduce saturated fat and caloric density. The dish is widely served in Singapore cafeterias, food courts, and casual dining restaurants as a working-lunch option. Texturally, the spaghetti is tender and the sauce is thick, moist, and mildly tangy from the tomato base. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 126 kcal, 5.74 g protein, 5.4 g fat, 12.77 g carbohydrate, 3.74 g sugar, 1.75 g dietary fibre, and 255 mg sodium. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free. The estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 (low) reflects the dominant contribution of cooked spaghetti. Clinically, the moderate sodium content (255 mg/100 g) warrants portion control for hypertensive patients, while the moderate carbohydrate load requires awareness for type 2 diabetes management. The tomato-based sauce is preferable to cream-based alternatives for cardiovascular health. The dish is classified as a processed, grain-staple pasta dish within the noodle/pasta-based dishes subgroup.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken bolognese?
Is chicken bolognese suitable for diabetics?
Chicken bolognese vs beef bolognese nutrition
Can I eat chicken bolognese with hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05060025) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest analogue (spaghetti with meat sauce) and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database published values for cooked spaghetti as the dominant carbohydrate source.
notes: Saturated fat (1.3 g/100 g) is an analogous estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Spaghetti with meat sauce' (NDB No. 27116) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry; this value should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish and are not available from a directly matching approved source; limited published data for this specific Singapore preparation. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white spaghetti (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008). This is a Western dish adapted for the Singapore market; local names for Singapore's official languages have been added reflecting common translations for 'Chicken Bolognese' or 'Chicken Pasta', as a direct culinary equivalent with a unique local name does not exist. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact code exists for chicken bolognese as a specific dish.

Iteration 3: Confirmed foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type are set to 'closest_parent' — valid per allowed list. All other sections preserved from iteration 2. No further structural changes required.
